worth_it_explanation: Content classification is the signal a buyer reads to decide whether a publisher's inventory matches a campaign and whether a brand-safety control should block it. When the categories passed are accurate, buyers target the right pages and apply their suitability rules correctly. When they are inflated, missing, or attached to the wrong page, buyers either avoid inventory that was safe or bid on inventory their brand-safety rules should have excluded, and the publisher loses demand it could have kept. The check is a review of the classification signals already flowing through PubMatic and the wrapper, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific category or page that can be corrected.
intro: Content classification is how a publisher tells buyers what a page is about. The categories travel in the bid request, usually as Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy codes carried in the OpenRTB content object, and buyers read them to target contextually and to apply brand-safety and brand-suitability rules. PubMatic, through its OpenWrap header-bidding wrapper and its server-side exchange, passes whatever classification a publisher sets, so the accuracy of that signal is the publisher's responsibility. These checks confirm that the categories sent reflect what the pages actually contain and that sensitive or mismatched content is labeled honestly rather than misrepresented to chase demand.

audit_checks.title: Content categories passed to buyers do not match the page
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Buyers read the content categories to target contextually and to apply brand-safety rules, so a category that does not match the page misdirects both. An inaccurate or stale category makes a campaign skip inventory that was a fit, or wins demand that a buyer's suitability rules would have excluded if the page had been labeled correctly, and the mismatch erodes the trust buyers place in the publisher's signal.

educate.base:
When a publisher offers inventory through PubMatic, the bid request carries a description of what the page is about. That description usually takes the form of Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy categories, a standard set of codes that gives buyers and sellers a common language for the subject of a page. The categories ride in the OpenRTB content object, in fields such as 'cat', 'sectioncat', and 'pagecat', and a buyer reads them to decide whether the page fits a campaign and whether a brand-safety rule should let the bid through.

The accuracy of that signal is the publisher's to set. PubMatic and its OpenWrap header-bidding wrapper pass the classification a publisher provides, whether that comes from the wrapper's first-party data configuration, a tag-level setting, or a category mapped to an ad unit. If the category is wrong, PubMatic still passes it, and the buyer acts on a description that does not match the page.

A mismatch hurts demand in both directions. A category that undersells the page, for example a recipe article tagged only with a broad 'Food & Drink' code when it sits inside a parenting section, can miss contextual campaigns that were targeting the more specific subject. A category that oversells or simply misstates the page, for example a finance code on a page that is really entertainment, can win bids from buyers whose suitability rules would have excluded the real subject, and those buyers lower their trust in the publisher's signal once they notice. Stale categories cause the same problem on pages that changed subject after they were first tagged.

A few signs point to categories that no longer match the pages:
  • The classification is set once at the site level and never varies by section or page, so every page claims the same broad subject
  • Section or page categories point to a taxonomy version a publisher no longer maintains, or to codes that were mapped years ago and not revisited
  • Spot-checking a sample of live pages against the categories in their bid requests turns up subjects that do not match
You may want to confirm that the categories flowing through PubMatic describe the pages as they are now. One approach is to pull the content categories from a sample of live bid requests, read each against the page it came from, and correct or refresh any that no longer fit.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, sections drift and pages get repurposed, so a category mapped when a section launched can describe a subject the section no longer covers. You may want to check the per-section and per-page categories against the live content, especially on evergreen pages that were tagged once and left, so the classification reflects what each section publishes today.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages carry a clear subject that buyers value, while editorial pages may share a single broad code. You may want to confirm the commercial pages send categories specific enough for contextual buyers to find them, rather than inheriting one site-wide classification that hides the higher-intent inventory.

learn_more.source_title: First Party Data for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for setting content categories in OpenWrap and the exchange is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the content categories flowing through PubMatic and the OpenWrap wrapper, then read a sample of them against the live pages they describe to see whether the classification matches.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open a sample of live pages across different sections and watch the bid requests in the Network tab as PubMatic and OpenWrap load.
Find the content categories in each request, for example the 'cat', 'sectioncat', and 'pagecat' fields in the OpenRTB content object, and note the codes being sent.
Read each set of codes against the page it came from and record whether the category matches the page's actual subject or describes something else.
Note any page where the category is missing, points to a taxonomy version no longer maintained, or describes a subject the page does not cover.

wrapper_config.label: OpenWrap configuration review
steps: Review where the content categories are set, whether at the site level, per section, per ad unit, or through a first-party data configuration in the wrapper.
Record which taxonomy version the categories map to and when the mapping was last revisited.
Note whether the classification varies by section and page or applies one broad category across all inventory.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a sample of live pages, the content categories sent in the bid request alongside the page's actual subject. A sample where the categories match the pages, vary by section where the content varies, and map to a current taxonomy version passes. A sample where categories describe the wrong subject, sit stale on pages that changed, or apply one broad site-level code across distinct content is a fail recorded with the pages and categories that diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the content categories sent for a sample of pages and the actual subject of each page above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the content categories {{site_name}} passes to buyers through PubMatic do not consistently match the pages they describe. Buyers read those categories to target contextually and to apply brand-safety rules, so a category that misstates the page either skips demand that was a fit or wins bids a buyer's suitability rules should have excluded, and buyers lower their trust in the signal once they notice. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the content categories from a sample of live bid requests, reading each against the page it came from, and correcting or refreshing the categories that no longer fit so the classification reflects the current content. After the correction, recheck a fresh sample to confirm the categories and the pages agree.

audit_checks.title: Sensitive content is mislabeled rather than flagged honestly
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Buyers rely on honest sensitive-content labeling to keep brands away from content they have agreed not to fund, so masking sensitive content as benign places ads against content a buyer's suitability rules would have blocked. When buyers detect it, they distrust the publisher's signal and can restrict or pause spend across the inventory, which puts demand at risk well beyond the mislabeled pages.

educate.base:
Some content sits in categories that advertisers treat as high-risk, for example explicit sexual content, hate speech, graphic violence, or terrorism. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) and the industry brand-suitability frameworks built on its Content Taxonomy give these a defined set of sensitive categories and risk tiers, so a buyer can decide which subjects a brand will fund and at which risk level. The system only works when the content is labeled honestly. A buyer's suitability rule can block a sensitive page only if the page tells the truth about what it contains.

For a publisher selling through PubMatic, the classification that flows out in the bid request is the label buyers act on. If a sensitive page is tagged with a benign category, or its sensitive nature is simply left out of the classification, the page reaches buyers as ordinary inventory. Their suitability rules never fire because the signal those rules read does not describe the real subject, and ads land against content the advertiser agreed not to fund.

The exposure runs deeper than a single bad placement. Brand-safety vendors and buyers verify content independently, and when their scan disagrees with the classification a publisher sent, the gap reads as a publisher passing inaccurate signals. The response is rarely limited to the offending pages. Buyers and demand partners can restrict, downgrade, or pause spend across the publisher's inventory until they trust the signal again, so a few mislabeled pages can cost demand on inventory that was never sensitive.

A few signs point to sensitive content that is not flagged honestly:
  • Pages with mature, violent, or otherwise sensitive subjects carry benign content categories or no sensitive-category signal at all
  • The classification has no way to express the sensitive categories or risk tiers that buyers' suitability rules depend on
  • A brand-safety vendor's scan of the inventory disagrees with the categories the publisher is sending on the same pages
It can be beneficial to label sensitive content for what it is rather than masking it to widen demand. One approach is to compare an independent brand-safety scan of the inventory against the categories being sent, then correct any page where the classification hides a sensitive subject so buyers' suitability rules can act on an honest signal.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, hard-news coverage of violence, conflict, or crime can carry a sensitive subject even when the journalism itself is responsible. You may want to label that content with the sensitive categories and risk tiers buyers expect, rather than tagging it as general news, so suitability-conscious buyers can decide for themselves while the honest signal protects the publisher's standing.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, most inventory is benign, but user-generated reviews, forums, or comment sections can host sensitive material the editorial pages do not. You may want to confirm those user-generated areas are classified for what they actually contain rather than inheriting the benign category of the surrounding commercial pages.

learn_more.source_title: Understanding Brand Safety and Brand Suitability in a Contemporary Media Environment

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for sensitive-content labeling and brand-safety controls is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review how sensitive content is classified, then compare the categories being sent against an independent read of the same pages to see whether sensitive subjects are flagged honestly or masked.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Identify pages or sections with mature, violent, or otherwise sensitive subjects, and load a sample while watching the bid requests in the Network tab.
Find the content categories in each request and record whether any sensitive category or risk-tier signal is present, or whether the page carries only benign codes.
Note any page whose sensitive subject is not reflected in the categories the bid request sends.

wrapper_config.label: OpenWrap configuration review
steps: Review whether the classification setup can express sensitive categories and risk tiers at all, or whether it only carries broad benign codes.
Record how sensitive sections, user-generated areas, and mature content are mapped, and whether that mapping reaches the bid request.
Note any gap where sensitive content has no path to an honest sensitive-category signal.

brand_safety_scan.label: Independent brand-safety read
steps: Run or obtain an independent content scan of a sample of the inventory, for example from a brand-safety vendor or a manual review against the industry sensitive-category definitions.
Compare the scan's read of each page against the categories the publisher is sending for that page.
Record any page where the independent read finds a sensitive subject that the publisher's classification does not reflect.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a sample of sensitive or potentially sensitive pages, the categories being sent alongside an independent read of the page's subject. A sample where sensitive content carries honest sensitive-category and risk-tier signals that agree with the independent read passes. A sample where sensitive pages carry benign codes, lack any sensitive-category signal, or disagree with an independent brand-safety scan is a fail recorded with the pages where the classification masks the real subject.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the categories sent for sensitive pages and an independent read of each page's subject above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that sensitive content on {{site_name}} is labeled as benign rather than flagged honestly in the classification sent to buyers. Buyers' brand-suitability rules can keep ads off high-risk subjects only when the content tells the truth about what it contains, so masking a sensitive page places ads against content the advertiser agreed not to fund, and once buyers detect the gap they can restrict spend across the inventory, not just the mislabeled pages. {{voice_recommend}} comparing an independent brand-safety scan against the categories being sent, then correcting any page where the classification hides a sensitive subject so the sensitive-category and risk-tier signals reach buyers honestly. After the correction, confirm a fresh scan agrees with the categories on the sensitive inventory.
